one_line_takeaway: CDD, a five-step decomposition prompt, substantially raises RAG accuracy on localized adversarial knowledge conflicts (e.g. 78.1% vs 63.0% Standard RAG macro on Gemini; 62.0% vs 15.0% on worst-case TruthfulQA misconception injection) and increases average-case accuracy on Claude models, but the paper's own causal-intervention tests show the stated resolution trace only demonstrably drives the answer on Gemini-2.5-Flash (64.1% sensitivity) -- on Claude the accuracy gain transfers without a measurable causal footprint in the trace, so the mechanism behind the improvement is model-specific and not established as a real causal chain-of-thought effect for Claude.
key_finding: Three main findings, all stated by the paper as its own results. (1) Context compliance is measurable and severe in the worst case: under TruthfulQA misconception injection (N=500), Standard RAG accuracy is 15.0% (+/-3.1%) versus CDD at 62.0% (+/-4.3%); the paper cautions the remaining ~38% for CDD is a mix of hedges/refusals/wrong answers, not a pure misconception-acceptance rate. (2) Accuracy transfer and causal-trace-sensitivity transfer dissociate across model families: on Gemini-2.5-Flash, CDD's Mistake-Injection causal sensitivity is 64.1% (accuracy drops from 78.1% to 28.0%), indicating the stated resolution trace strongly drives the final answer; on the Claude family (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) mistake-injection sensitivities are all within [-3%, +7%] (point estimates, no paired significance test) even though CDD's adversarial accuracy gain transfers positively to all three Claude models (+2.2/+4.6/+2.6 percentage points over Standard RAG). The paper explicitly concludes this means the Claude-family accuracy gains cannot be attributed to the resolution trace causally driving the answer, based on these point estimates alone. (3) On the Epi-Scale adversarial records (Gemini-2.5-Flash, N=2,250), CDD reaches 78.1% macro accuracy vs. 63.0% for Standard RAG and 73.5% for the Conflict-aware RAG instruction-only baseline; the CDD margin over Conflict-aware RAG is clearest and CI-non-overlapping on Entity Swap (88.0% +/-2.7 vs 79.3% +/-3.3) and Logical Contradiction (83.2% +/-3.1 vs 75.4% +/-3.6), while Temporal Shift (71.3% vs 70.0%) and Distractor Evidence (69.9% vs 69.2%) gains have overlapping CIs and are not claimed as significant. Ablation: removing Step 4 (Premise Isolation) drops overall macro accuracy to 65.1%, removing Step 3 (Divergence Check) to 66.0%; the length-matched Sham CoT variant scores only 40.1% overall, below full CDD's 78.1%, supporting that the specific decomposition content (not just extra generation length) drives the gain. A separate mixed clean/adversarial calibration shows CDD and Standard RAG are statistically tied on harmonic-mean average-case accuracy (72.23% vs 72.33%, bootstrap p=0.53), because CDD trades roughly 18 points of clean-sample accuracy (67.2% vs Standard RAG's 84.9%) for its adversarial robustness gain -- i.e., CDD is not a universally better prompt, only a conflict-specific one. CDD-alpha (tau=0.7, routing 30% of samples to full CDD) reaches 68.5% adversarial accuracy, only about 1.0pp above the 67.5% expected under random 30% routing, so the paper presents it as an illustrative operating point rather than a demonstrated compute/accuracy win.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Context-Driven Decomposition (CDD), a prompt-level, inference-time diagnostic intervention for RAG systems under knowledge conflict (context vs. parametric prior). CDD decomposes a conflicted query-answer instance into five explicit steps: (1) extract the answer implied by the retrieved context, (2) elicit the model's parametric answer (elicited with context already visible, so it is not a context-free prior), (3) compare the two and check for divergence, (4) if they conflict, isolate the specific contradictory premise and judge whether it violates established history/science, and (5) resolve the conflict into a final answer. The paper frames this as making an otherwise implicit 'source arbitration' process observable and testable, rather than as a production defense. It also introduces CDD-alpha, a compute-aware routing variant that uses a prompted Gemini-2.5-Flash contradiction-score gate (threshold tau) to route only high-conflict samples to the full CDD trace, defaulting other samples to Standard RAG. The paper's own benchmark, Epi-Scale (2,250 base examples from HotpotQA, Natural Questions, and FEVER, each instantiated as a clean and an LLM-generated adversarial record for 4,500 total records across four perturbation types: Entity Swap, Temporal Shift, Logical Contradiction, Distractor Evidence), is built specifically to stress-test this diagnostic.
threat_model: This is not an attack paper and proposes no new attack; it studies robustness to misleading/adversarial retrieved context in RAG rather than proposing a threat model of an external attacker compromising a retrieval corpus. The paper's own adversarial conditions are: (1) synthetic knowledge-conflict perturbations (Entity Swap, Temporal Shift, Logical Contradiction, Distractor Evidence) generated by an LLM (gemini-2.5-flash) against Epi-Scale base examples, and (2) a worst-case TruthfulQA misconception-injection probe in which the most common human misconception is placed directly in the retrieved context as an upper-bound compliance test. The paper explicitly frames the TruthfulQA setting as testing 'what happens when retrieval returns a maximally misleading single context, not how often such contexts are retrieved by BM25 or DPR' -- i.e., it deliberately does not model a realistic retrieval-corpus poisoning threat model (that class of attack, e.g. PoisonedRAG, is cited only as related work, not studied here). The paper also flags a same-model-bias confound: because the primary evaluation model (Gemini-2.5-Flash) both generated the adversarial perturbations and served as the CDD-alpha contradiction gate, perturbation difficulty may be calibrated to that model's own weaknesses; the Claude-family reruns partially address this since Claude did not generate the perturbations.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section is extensive and should be treated as load-bearing, not boilerplate: (1) the clearest causal-sensitivity signal is Gemini-specific (64.1%), with Claude-family causal coupling unresolved (point estimates in [-3%,+7%], no paired significance/power/equivalence analysis); (2) the TruthfulQA 15.0%/62.0% figures come from an explicit worst-case single-misconception injection, not organic BM25/DPR retrieval, so they upper-bound rather than measure typical deployed misinformation exposure; (3) the Standard RAG vs. CDD prompt comparison has a baseline-instruction asymmetry (Standard RAG's prompt doesn't tell the model to check for conflicts, CDD's does), partially but not fully addressed by the Conflict-aware RAG baseline; (4) the CDD-alpha threshold tau=0.7 was chosen by eyeballing a 100-sample histogram from the same evaluation pool rather than a held-out dev set or a tau sweep; (5) Mistake Injection conflates a false factual claim with a behavioral directive, so it cannot cleanly isolate factual-content effects from instruction-following effects; (6) belief-revision framing (parametric prior vs. contextual posterior as probability distributions) is explicitly stated to be conceptual only -- no token-level/logit divergence (e.g., JSD) is actually computed, since the models are closed-API; (7) Step 2's 'parametric' answer is elicited with the context already visible, so it is not a context-free prior, weakening the prior/posterior separation the framework rests on; (8) results are single-run, temperature-0.0, closed-API evaluations, which the paper itself notes are not guaranteed deterministic and are subject to undisclosed serving-infrastructure drift; (9) same-model bias in perturbation generation (Gemini generated the adversarial perturbations and also serves as the CDD-alpha gate) is only partially mitigated by the Claude reruns; (10) the perturbation-validity manual audit covered only 200 records (50 per subgroup, 92% valid-conflict rate) with no inter-annotator agreement measured.

datasets_used: Epi-Scale (paper's own benchmark; 2,250 base examples drawn evenly from HotpotQA, Natural Questions, and FEVER, each instantiated as a clean-context and an adversarial-context record for 4,500 total records; adversarial records split across Entity Swap, Temporal Shift, Logical Contradiction, Distractor Evidence, ~562-563 per type)
TruthfulQA (Lin et al. 2022) -- 500-instance misconception-injection probe using the most common human misconception as retrieved context
HotpotQA (Yang et al. 2018), Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al. 2019), FEVER (Thorne et al. 2018) -- source pools for Epi-Scale base examples
quantitative_results: Epi-Scale adversarial macro accuracy (Gemini-2.5-Flash, N=2,250): ClosedBook 44.7% (+/-2.0), Standard RAG 63.0% (+/-2.0), Vanilla CoT 63.7% (+/-2.0), Reflection Prompting 67.0% (+/-1.9), Prompt-Filtered RAG 66.3% (+/-2.0), Conflict-aware RAG 73.5% (+/-1.8), CDD 78.1% (+/-1.7)
Entity Swap accuracy: CDD 88.0% (+/-2.7) vs Conflict-aware RAG 79.3% (+/-3.3) vs Standard RAG 58.4% (+/-4.1)
Logical Contradiction accuracy: CDD 83.2% (+/-3.1) vs Conflict-aware RAG 75.4% (+/-3.6) vs Standard RAG 56.0% (+/-4.1)
Temporal Shift / Distractor Evidence: CDD 71.3%/69.9% vs Conflict-aware RAG 70.0%/69.2% (overlapping CIs, no significance claimed)
TruthfulQA-500 worst-case misconception injection: Standard RAG 15.0% (+/-3.1%) vs CDD 62.0% (+/-4.3%)
Cross-model replication adversarial accuracy (N=2,250 each): Claude Haiku Standard RAG 79.0% -> CDD 81.2% (+/-1.6); Claude Sonnet 76.0% -> 80.6% (+/-1.6); Claude Opus 79.4% -> 82.0% (+/-1.6)

Mistake-injection causal sensitivity: Gemini-2.5-Flash CDD 64.1% (accuracy 78.1% -> 28.0%); Vanilla CoT on Gemini 4.1% (63.7% -> 61.1%); Claude-family CDD sensitivities within [-3%, +7%] (Sonnet and Opus negative, i.e. accuracy rose slightly under corruption); Claude Sonnet Vanilla CoT sensitivity 20.3% (reported as an isolated outlier, not over-interpreted)

Average-case (mixed clean/adversarial) harmonic mean: CDD 72.23% vs Standard RAG 72.33% (bootstrap p=0.53, statistically tied); component breakdown CDD 67.2% clean / 78.1% adversarial vs Standard RAG 84.9% clean / 63.0% adversarial
Ablation (Gemini, overall macro): Full CDD 78.1%, w/o Step 4 (Isolation) 65.1%, w/o Step 3 (Divergence) 66.0%, Length-Matched Sham CoT 40.1%

CDD-alpha (tau=0.7): routes 30% of samples to full CDD, 70% to Standard RAG; achieves 68.5% adversarial accuracy, ~1.0pp above the 67.5% expected from random 30% routing (0.7x63.0 + 0.3x78.1); relative compute ~1.6x Standard RAG (0.7x1 + 0.3x3)
Perturbation-generation validity audit: 92% valid conflict generation rate (manual spot check, 50 records per subgroup, 200 records total)
baselines_compared: Reflection Prompting (prompt-only approximation inspired by Self-RAG, Asai et al. 2024 -- explicitly not the trained Self-RAG system)
Prompt-Filtered RAG (prompt-only self-filtering baseline inspired by document-filtering RAG, Yoran et al. 2024 -- not a trained NLI filter)

Conflict-aware RAG (instruction-only baseline: tells the model to override context that conflicts with established facts, used to isolate the effect of explicit decomposition from mere conflict-awareness instruction)
Length-Matched Sham CoT (structurally similar 5-step prompt with semantically vacuous steps, used as an ablation control for generation length rather than as a headline baseline)
